name: fellow capitoline deity
shortDescription: A deity who was part of the Capitoline Triad, alongside Jupiter and Minerva, signifying their importance in Roman state religion.
description: The fellow Capitoline deity refers to either Jupiter or Juno, depending on the context, as Minerva was part of the Capitoline Triad with both. The Capitoline Triad was a group of three deities who were worshipped in the Temple of Jupiter Optimus Maximus on the Capitoline Hill in Rome. They were central to Roman state religion. Jupiter was the king of the gods, Juno his wife and queen, and Minerva their daughter. Their worship was crucial for ensuring the prosperity and protection of Rome. The specific choice of deity, whether Jupiter or Juno, depends on the context in which Minerva is being discussed. Jupiter represented law, order, and victory, while Juno embodied marriage, motherhood, and the well-being of the Roman state. Their inclusion with Minerva in the Capitoline Triad highlighted the importance of wisdom, strength, and divine favor in the Roman world. The Capitoline Triad was a symbol of Roman power and religious devotion.

sources.title: A Dictionary of Roman Religion
sources.author: Lesley Adkins and Roy A. Adkins
